mnemonics.title: Instruction Cycle Steps
mnemonics.content: FETCH-DECODE-EXECUTE: First GET the instruction (Fetch), then UNDERSTAND it (Decode), finally DO it (Execute). Like: Go to kitchen → Read recipe → Cook food.

mnemonics.title: CPU Registers
mnemonics.content: PC TELLS WHERE TO GO (Program Counter), IR SHOWS WHAT TO DO (Instruction Register), ACC HOLDS THE ANSWER (Accumulator). Think: 'PICA' = PC + IR + ACC + more registers.

mnemonics.title: Bus Types
mnemonics.content: DATA moves, ADDRESS locates, CONTROL commands. D-A-C: Data = what's moved, Address = where to, Control = how to do it.

mnemonics.title: Memory Hierarchy Speed
mnemonics.content: REGISTERS ARE THE FASTEST (like your thinking), CACHE IS QUICK (like short-term memory), RAM IS WORKING (like desk), STORAGE IS SLOW (like filing cabinet).

acronyms.usage: Data transfer method bypassing CPU between I/O and memory
Register holding address of next instruction
Holds data being transferred to/from memory
Generates control signals for instruction execution

visualTips.visualization: Draw a circular flow: FETCH (memory → IR) → DECODE (IR → CU) → EXECUTE (ALU/CU performs action) → back to FETCH. Like a factory assembly line.
Draw inverted pyramid: Top (smallest, fastest): Registers → Cache → RAM → Secondary Storage (bottom, largest, slowest). Label each with typical size and access time.
Draw CPU as a box with three internal boxes: Control Unit (top), ALU (middle), Registers (bottom). Show arrows for data flow between them and to external memory bus.
Draw central memory box connected to CPU box via bus. Show single path for both instructions and data. Show I/O connected to bus. Like a city with one main road for all traffic.

analogies.analogy: CPU is like the brain processing thoughts, memory is like notebooks storing information. Brain fetches from notebooks to think, stores results back.
Cache is like a desk organizer. You keep frequently used items (papers, pens) on desk for quick access rather than searching through filing cabinet every time.
Interrupts are like phone calls while you're working. You pause your task to answer (handle interrupt), then resume where you left off.
Bus is like a city's road system - data bus is delivery trucks, address bus is GPS/location tags, control bus is traffic signals coordinating movement.
DMA is like a delivery service that drops packages at your door without you (CPU) having to go pick them up each time - you stay free to do other work.
IR is like a chef's recipe card currently being read - the current instruction telling the kitchen what to cook right now.
